rationale: Y's approval reasoning independently reconstructs the ground-truth reviewer's core justification (codes.audio now matches the postprocess writer) and its top non-blocking comment (assertions.py:658) closely mirrors the human's key concern — the containment fallback silently loosens the 'strict unless opt-in' contract for all callers — backed by a concrete counterexample ('hello' vs 'hello world goodbye'), plus a valid novel observation that min() makes short_text nearly always true. X's five findings (unpopped legacy audio key, missing test length-guard, duplicated magic number, undocumented thresholds, __init__ bypass) are all grounded but low-severity nits that never touch the ground truth's actual gaps (stale docstring, legacy-vs-nested check ordering/live-producer doubt), so its recall is weak despite being reasonably actionable.
